ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese banh mi with grilled beef in betel leaf. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (meat sandwich) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for banh mi or Vietnamese-style beef-filled baguette.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese-Singaporean sandwich preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard banh mi roll with grilled beef in betel leaf, approximately 220 g, including bread, filling, condiments, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef sandwich composite; adapted with betel leaf beef roll component data from USDA SR Legacy ground beef (cooked) and Vietnamese banh mi bread profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bánh Mì Bò Lá Lốt exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component analysis: Vietnamese-style baguette (~40% of serving weight, ~260 kcal/100g), grilled ground beef in betel leaf (~45% of serving weight, ~215 kcal/100g per USDA FDC #174032 ground beef 80% lean cooked), mayonnaise (~5%), pickled vegetables and condiments (~10%). Weighted composite per 100g calculated from these proportions. Sodium elevated due to fish sauce and chilli sauce. Values represent a reasonable central estimate; actual restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread / baguette analogue; mixed meat sandwich composite estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bánh Mì Bò Lá Lốt. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle being a white wheat baguette (GI ~55–60 per University of Sydney GI Database for white bread/baguette). The protein and fat content from beef and mayonnaise moderately attenuate glycemic response. GI of 55 (medium) is a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 53.9) / 100 ≈ 29.6, where carbohydrate per 220g serving = 24.5 × 2.2 = 53.9g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, chilli sauce, and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100g (roughly 1,144 mg per serving), which represents nearly 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce and condiments may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from white wheat baguette as primary carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this sandwich has a medium glycemic index (~55) and a high glycemic load per serving (~29.6) driven by the white baguette; portion control is important, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or choosing a smaller portion can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and mayonnaise, and elevated sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents moderate concern due to saturated fat (~3.2g per 100g) from the grilled beef and mayonnaise, combined with high sodium from fish sauce and pickled condiments; limiting portion size and requesting lighter condiments is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Beef in Betel Leaf Banh Mi (Bánh Mì Bò Lá Lốt) is a Vietnamese-origin sandwich served in Singapore, combining the classic Vietnamese bánh mì format with the distinctive flavour of bò lá lốt — seasoned ground beef wrapped in betel leaf (Piper lolot) and grilled over charcoal or an open flame. The betel leaf imparts a mildly peppery, aromatic, slightly bitter flavour to the beef, which is typically seasoned with lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, and spices before grilling. The filling is placed inside a light, crispy Vietnamese-style French baguette and layered with homemade mayonnaise, a proprietary fish sauce dressing, chilli sauce, pickled daikon and carrot, fresh cilantro, and sliced fresh chilli. The result is a sandwich with contrasting textures — a crisp, airy crust giving way to tender, smoky beef — and a complex flavour profile balancing savoury, tangy, spicy, and herbal notes. Nutritionally, this sandwich is a moderate-energy food (~230 kcal per 100g) providing meaningful protein (~11.5g per 100g) from the beef, moderate carbohydrates (~24.5g per 100g) from the baguette, and notable sodium (~520 mg per 100g) from the condiment-heavy preparation. The dish is clinically relevant for sodium-sensitive individuals and those managing blood glucose due to its white bread base and high glycemic load per serving. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or fish allergies. In Singapore, this dish is associated with Vietnamese-Singaporean cuisine and is available at specialty Vietnamese restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bánh Mì Bò Lá Lốt was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FDC data for cooked ground beef, white baguette, mayonnaise, and condiments. GI is estimated by analogy to white bread/baguette from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is systematic and defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated composites; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, beef fat content, bread size, and condiment quantities. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this food type in the component sources used. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are estimated from USDA FDC component data and are marked accordingly. The dish is served at Co Chung Restaurant (Lorong Telok) in Singapore as of the field survey data collection date. Price recorded as SGD 20.58. The cuisine classification is Vietnamese-Singaporean reflecting the dish's Vietnamese origin and Singapore serving context. Halal status is unconfirmed as fish sauce sourcing and beef certification were not verified. GI set to 55 (medium) based on white baguette analogue from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per serving ~29.6 based on 220g serving size.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Grilled Beef in Betel Leaf Banh Mi' is precise and non-promotional. Vietnamese name 'Bánh Mì Bò Lá Lốt' present. All mandatory Singaporean-cuisine language fields populated: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Vietnamese.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal 230.0, protein_g 11.5, fat_g 9.8, carbohydrate_g 24.5. Source is USDA FoodData Central with component estimation methodology clearly documented. No zero macros requiring justification.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.5, sodium_mg 520.0, saturated_fat_g 3.2, sugar_g 3.0 — all present as numeric values and plausible for a beef sandwich with condiments.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 55 (numeric, >0), gi_category 'medium', glycemic_load_per_serving 29.6, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate basis. All four GI fields present and valid.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FKP (meat sandwich) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT set to none with gap note. No codes invented; all match_type values are from the approved set (exact, closest_parent, none).

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains the food identity, preparation method, taste and texture profile, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutritional characteristics. Common user queries and meal context populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, glycemic load from white baguette), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat and sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name fields correctly set.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases list contains 14 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese script, romanized forms, Chinese script (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. aliases_by_language is fully populated and every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. Consistency check passed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Previous iteration produced invalid JSON (empty output from gemini). Rebuilt complete valid JSON from scratch.
Iteration 3: Verified JSON validity, aliases consistency, ontology match_type values, sentinel value documentation, GI numeric validity, multilingual coverage, and all hard gates before finalising.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FDC with clear methodology documentation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (white baguette). All mandatory Singaporean-cuisine multilingual fields are populated. Aliases are consistent between flat list and aliases_by_language. Ontology uses only approved match_type values. All -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. No zero core macros. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
